## Module Content

```markdown
# Tomorrow - Future Tense

*Talking About What Will Happen*

## Вступ (Introduction)

You already know how to talk about the present — **Я читаю книгу** (I'm reading a book). In the last module, you learned to look back at **вчора** (yesterday) and say what happened — **Я читав книгу** (I read a book). Now it's time to look forward to **завтра** (tomorrow).

Think of it as a timeline you've been building:

- **Вчора** я читав книгу. — Yesterday I read a book.
- **Сьогодні** я читаю книгу. — Today I'm reading a book.
- **Завтра** я **буду** читати книгу. — Tomorrow I will read a book.

Notice the pattern? The verb **читати** stays the same — what changes is the helper word before it. That little word **буду** is your key to the future.

There's a phrase Ukrainians use that captures the spirit of looking forward: **«Завтра буде новий день.»** — "Tomorrow will be a new day." You'll hear this in messages, in conversations, in songs. It's an expression of hope and resilience — no matter what happened today, tomorrow brings something new. And it's also a perfect example of how the future tense works: **буде** (will be) + the rest of the sentence.

By the end of this module, you'll be able to:
- Form the future tense using **буду** + infinitive
- Talk about your plans for tomorrow, next week, and beyond
- Express intentions using **хотіти** and **збиратися**
- Use time expressions like **завтра вранці** and **наступного тижня**

[!tip] **Quick win!**
You already know dozens of verbs in their infinitive form — **читати**, **писати**, **говорити**, **робити**. To make any of them future tense, just add the right form of **буду** in front. You're about to unlock the future with words you already know!

## Презентація граматики (Grammar Presentation)

### Складений майбутній час (Compound Future)

The compound future tense in Ukrainian works like a team of two words: a form of **бути** (to be) + the infinitive of the main verb. The helper verb changes to match the person — the main verb stays in the infinitive.

<!-- adapted from: Кравцова 2021, Grade 4, p.109 -->

| Особа | Однина | | Множина | |
|-------|--------|------|---------|------|
| 1st | я **буду** читати | I will read | ми **будемо** читати | we will read |
| 2nd | ти **будеш** читати | you will read | ви **будете** читати | you (pl.) will read |
| 3rd | він/вона **буде** читати | he/she will read | вони **будуть** читати | they will read |

The main verb never changes — only **бути** conjugates. Here's that same pattern with a few verbs you know:

- Я **буду** працювати. — I will work.
- Ти **будеш** говорити. — You will speak.
- Вона **буде** слухати музику. — She will listen to music.
- Ми **будемо** вивчати українську. — We will study Ukrainian.
- Ви **будете** читати книгу. — You will read a book.
- Вони **будуть** грати. — They will play.

<!-- adapted from: Варзацька 2021, Grade 4, p.133 -->

[!warning] **Learner Error — Aspect Conflict!**
**Буду** works ONLY with imperfective verbs (the ones you've been using — **читати**, **писати**, **робити**). Never combine **буду** with a perfective verb:
- **буду читати** ✅ (I will read / I will be reading)
- ~~буду прочитати~~ ❌ (incorrect — aspect clash!)

At A1, stick with the verbs you already know. They're all imperfective, so you're safe. If the infinitive ends in **-ати**, **-ити**, **-увати**, **-ти** and you've learned it in present tense — it works with **буду**.

### Synthetic Future (FYI — Recognition Only)

You may encounter a shorter future form in songs, news, or signs: **працюватиму**, **читатимеш**, **робитиме**. This is the synthetic future — the endings **-му**, **-меш**, **-ме**, **-мемо**, **-мете**, **-муть** are attached directly to the infinitive.

- **Я працюватиму** = Я буду працювати (I will work)
- **Він читатиме** = Він буде читати (He will read)

[!note] **Just recognise, don't memorise.**
Both forms mean the same thing. At this stage, use the compound form (**буду** + infinitive) when you speak and write. You'll learn to produce the synthetic form later — for now, just know it exists so you're not surprised when you see it.

## Лексика та культурний контекст (Vocabulary and Culture)

### Future Time Expressions

You already know **вчора** (yesterday) and **сьогодні** (today). Now let's add the forward-looking words:

- **Завтра** буде гарний день. — Tomorrow will be a nice day.
- **Завтра вранці** я буду пити каву. — Tomorrow morning I'll drink coffee.
- **Завтра вдень** ми будемо працювати. — Tomorrow afternoon we'll work.
- **Завтра ввечері** вони будуть читати. — Tomorrow evening they'll read.
- **Післязавтра** я буду вдома. — The day after tomorrow I'll be at home.
- **Скоро** буде літо. — Soon it will be summer.
- **Потім** я буду їсти. — Then I'll eat.
- **Пізніше** ми будемо говорити. — Later we'll talk.

### Конструкція «наступного тижня/року»

In English you say "next week" — the word "next" stays the same. In Ukrainian, **наступний** changes its ending when used with time expressions, and the noun changes too:

- **наступного тижня** — next week
- **наступного року** — next year
- **наступного разу** — next time
- **наступного місяця** — next month

[!warning] **Learner Error — Don't translate word-for-word!**
English "next week" keeps both words in their base form. Ukrainian uses a special form for both words: **наступного тижня** (not ~~наступний тиждень~~). Memorise these as fixed phrases — they use the Genitive case, which you'll study in more detail later.

Let's see them in sentences:

- **Наступного тижня** я буду багато працювати. — Next week I'll work a lot.
- **Наступного року** ми будемо вивчати більше. — Next year we'll study more.

### Plans and Intentions

Ukrainian has three ways to talk about the future, each with a different level of certainty:

| Expression | Meaning | Certainty |
|-----------|---------|-----------|
| **буду** + infinitive | I will... | confident plan |
| **збираюся** + infinitive | I'm going to... | intention |
| **хочу** + infinitive | I want to... | desire |

Let's compare:

- Я **буду** читати книгу. — I will read a book. (it's decided)
- Я **збираюся** читати книгу. — I'm going to read a book. (that's my plan)
- Я **хочу** читати книгу. — I want to read a book. (wish, not sure yet)

And there are two more verbs for softer future plans:

- Я **сподіваюся**, що завтра буде тепло. — I hope that tomorrow will be warm.
- Вона **мріє** про подорож. — She dreams about a trip.
- Ми **плануємо** вивчати українську. — We plan to study Ukrainian.

> **(At the office / На роботі)**
>
> — Які у тебе **плани** на вихідні? — What are your plans for the weekend?
> — Я **збираюся** їхати до Львова. — I'm going to go to Lviv.
> — О, добре! А **скоро** їдеш? — Oh, nice! Going soon?
> — **Завтра вранці**. А ти? — Tomorrow morning. And you?
> — Я **буду** відпочивати вдома. — I'll rest at home.
> — А **потім**? — And then?
> — **Пізніше** буду читати книгу. — Later I'll read a book.

[!culture] **«Не кажи "гоп", поки не перескочиш»**
Literally: "Don't say 'hop' until you've jumped over." Ukrainians tend to be cautious about announcing plans too confidently. You might hear someone use **збираюся** or **сподіваюся** even when they're quite sure — it's considered polite not to tempt fate. This is why knowing the difference between **буду** (certain), **збираюся** (planning), and **хочу** (hoping) matters beyond just grammar!

The word **план** is very common in everyday conversation:

- **мати плани** — to have plans
- **складати план** — to make a plan
- **плани на майбутнє** — plans for the future

> **(At a café / У кафе)**
>
> — Ти маєш **плани** на завтра? — Do you have plans for tomorrow?
> — Так! Я **хочу** їхати до парку. — Yes! I want to go to the park.
> — А **наступного тижня**? — And next week?
> — **Наступного тижня** буду працювати. — Next week I'll work.
> — Я теж. **Мрію** про канікули! — Me too. I dream about a holiday!
> — **Сподіваюся**, **скоро** будуть канікули. — I hope there'll be a holiday soon.

## Практика та підсумок (Practice and Summary)

Let's put everything together. You now have all the pieces to talk about your future:

**Step 1: Choose your time expression**
- **Завтра**, **завтра вранці**, **скоро**, **потім**, **пізніше**, **наступного тижня**

**Step 2: Add the right form of бути**
- **Я буду**, **ти будеш**, **він/вона буде**, **ми будемо**, **ви будете**, **вони будуть**

**Step 3: Add the infinitive**
- **читати**, **працювати**, **їхати**, **вивчати**, **відпочивати**...

Try building your own sentences with this formula:

- **Завтра** я **буду** _____________.
- **Наступного тижня** ми **будемо** _____________.
- Я **збираюся** _____________ **завтра ввечері**.

> **(Planning a trip / Планування подорожі)**
>
> — Давай **планувати** вихідні! — Let's plan the weekend!
> — Добре. У суботу я **буду** ходити до музею. — OK. On Saturday I'll go to the museum.
> — А я **збираюся** їхати на екскурсію. — And I'm going to go on a tour.
> — У неділю ми **будемо** разом? — On Sunday will we be together?
> — Так! Я **планую** бути вдома. — Yes! I plan to be at home.
> — А **потім** **будемо** дивитися фільм? — And then we'll watch a film?
> — **Сподіваюся**! Буде чудово. — I hope so! It'll be wonderful.

[!practice] **Self-check — Can you do these?**
- ✅ Conjugate **бути** in the future for all six persons
- ✅ Form sentences like **Я буду читати завтра**
- ✅ Use time expressions: **завтра вранці**, **наступного тижня**, **скоро**
- ✅ Choose between **буду** (certain), **збираюся** (plan), and **хочу** (wish)
- ✅ Recognise the synthetic future form: **працюватиму** = **буду працювати**

You've just unlocked the future tense! You can now talk about what you did yesterday, what you're doing today, and what you'll do tomorrow. **Завтра буде новий день** — and you're ready for it.

---

**Recommended video:** [Making plans — Future tense in Ukrainian (ULP 1-28)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eoIgON1cPcI) — Watch Anna and her guest plan their weekend using exactly the structures you've just learned.

**Recommended video:** [Quick Ukrainian Class #18: Future Verb Conjugation](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OF-4hhOcx58) — A quick walkthrough of **бути** conjugation with clear examples.

**Further reading:** [Future Tense in Ukrainian](https://www.ukrainianlessons.com/grammar-future/) — A comprehensive guide covering both compound and synthetic forms.

**Podcast:** [ULP S3 Ep96: Perfective future tense](https://www.ukrainianlessons.com/episode96/) — Listen to future tense forms in a natural conversation about New Year celebrations.
```

---

## Activities

```yaml
- type: fill-in
  title: Complete the Future Tense
  instruction: Choose the correct form to complete each sentence about future plans.
  items:
    - sentence: "Я ___ читати книгу завтра."
      answer: "буду"
      options: ["буду", "будеш", "буде", "будуть"]
      explanation: "Я (I) takes the form буду."
    - sentence: "Ти ___ працювати завтра вранці."
      answer: "будеш"
      options: ["буду", "будеш", "будемо", "будете"]
      explanation: "Ти (you, singular) takes the form будеш."
    - sentence: "Вона ___ слухати музику ввечері."
      answer: "буде"
      options: ["буду", "будеш", "буде", "будуть"]
      explanation: "Вона (she) takes the form буде."
    - sentence: "Ми ___ вивчати українську наступного тижня."
      answer: "будемо"
      options: ["будемо", "будете", "будуть", "буду"]
      explanation: "Ми (we) takes the form будемо."
    - sentence: "Ви ___ читати книгу пізніше."
      answer: "будете"
      options: ["будемо", "будете", "будуть", "будеш"]
      explanation: "Ви (you, plural/formal) takes the form будете."
    - sentence: "Вони ___ грати у парку."
      answer: "будуть"
      options: ["буде", "будемо", "будете", "будуть"]
      explanation: "Вони (they) takes the form будуть."
    - sentence: "Він ___ говорити потім."
      answer: "буде"
      options: ["буду", "будеш", "буде", "будемо"]
      explanation: "Він (he) takes the form буде."
    - sentence: "___ я буду пити каву."
      answer: "Завтра вранці"
      options: ["Завтра вранці", "Наступного року", "Вчора", "Сьогодні"]
      explanation: "Завтра вранці means tomorrow morning — a future time expression."
    - sentence: "Я ___ їхати до Львова."
      answer: "збираюся"
      options: ["збираюся", "збирається", "збираємося", "збираєтеся"]
      explanation: "Збираюся (I'm going to) expresses an intention with я."
    - sentence: "Вона ___ про подорож."
      answer: "мріє"
      options: ["мріє", "мрію", "мріємо", "мрієш"]
      explanation: "Мріє is the third person singular form of мріяти (to dream)."
    - sentence: "Я ___ знати більше про Україну."
      answer: "хочу"
      options: ["хочу", "хоче", "хочемо", "хочете"]
      explanation: "Хочу (I want) is used with я to express a desire."
    - sentence: "___ буде літо."
      answer: "Скоро"
      options: ["Скоро", "Вчора", "Завтра вранці", "Наступного тижня"]
      explanation: "Скоро means soon — it will be summer soon."
    - sentence: "Я буду їсти, а ___ буду читати."
      answer: "потім"
      options: ["потім", "вчора", "завтра", "скоро"]
      explanation: "Потім means then/afterwards, showing sequence of actions."
    - sentence: "___ ми будемо працювати вдень."
      answer: "Завтра"
      options: ["Завтра", "Вчора", "Потім", "Пізніше"]
      explanation: "Завтра (tomorrow) is the most common future time expression."
    - sentence: "Ми ___ вивчати більше наступного року."
      answer: "будемо"
      options: ["буду", "будеш", "будемо", "будуть"]
      explanation: "Ми (we) takes the form будемо."
    - sentence: "___ я буду вдома."
      answer: "Післязавтра"
      options: ["Післязавтра", "Вчора", "Сьогодні", "Потім"]
      explanation: "Післязавтра means the day after tomorrow."
    - sentence: "Я ___, що завтра буде тепло."
      answer: "сподіваюся"
      options: ["сподіваюся", "сподівається", "сподіваємося", "збираюся"]
      explanation: "Сподіваюся (I hope) expresses hope about the future."
    - sentence: "Ми ___ подорож на вихідні."
      answer: "плануємо"
      options: ["плануємо", "планую", "планує", "плануєте"]
      explanation: "Плануємо (we plan) is the ми form of планувати."
    - sentence: "___ будемо дивитися фільм?"
      answer: "Потім"
      options: ["Потім", "Вчора", "Зараз", "Сьогодні"]
      explanation: "Потім (then/afterwards) connects future actions in sequence."
    - sentence: "___ ми будемо разом."
      answer: "Пізніше"
      options: ["Пізніше", "Вчора", "Раніше", "Давно"]
      explanation: "Пізніше means later — another future time expression."
```

---

## Vocabulary

```yaml
items:
  - lemma: "завтра"
    translation: "tomorrow"
    pos: "adverb"
    usage: "завтра вранці, завтра вдень, завтра ввечері"
    notes: "High frequency (Top 100). The most common future time marker."
  - lemma: "бути"
    translation: "to be"
    pos: "verb"
    aspect: "imperfective"
    usage: "я буду, ти будеш, він/вона буде, ми будемо, ви будете, вони будуть"
    notes: "Future tense helper verb. Used with infinitive to form compound future."
  - lemma: "наступний"
    translation: "next"
    pos: "adjective"
    usage: "наступного тижня, наступного року, наступного разу"
    notes: "Changes form in time expressions (Genitive case)."
  - lemma: "план"
    translation: "plan"
    pos: "noun"
    gender: "m"
    usage: "мати плани, складати план, плани на майбутнє"
    notes: "High frequency in everyday conversation."
  - lemma: "хотіти"
    translation: "to want"
    pos: "verb"
    aspect: "imperfective"

    pos: "verb"

  - lemma: "скоро"

  - lemma: "тиждень"
    translation: "week"
    pos: "noun"
    gender: "m"
    usage: "наступного тижня"
    notes: "Changes to тижня in Genitive (used in time expressions)."
  - lemma: "рік"
    translation: "year"
    pos: "noun"
    gender: "m"
    usage: "наступного року"
    notes: "Changes to року in Genitive (used in time expressions)."
  - lemma: "сподіватися"
    translation: "to hope"
    pos: "verb"
    aspect: "imperfective"
    usage: "сподіваюся, що все буде добре"
    notes: "Softer than буду — expresses hope rather than certainty."
  - lemma: "мріяти"
    translation: "to dream"
    pos: "verb"
    aspect: "imperfective"
    usage: "мріяти про відпустку, мрію про подорож"
    notes: "Used with про + Accusative for dreams/aspirations."
  - lemma: "планувати"
    translation: "to plan"
    pos: "verb"
    aspect: "imperfective"
    usage: "планувати час, планувати подорож"
    notes: "More formal than збиратися for expressing future intentions."
  - lemma: "пізніше"
    translation: "later"
    pos: "adverb"
    usage: "трохи пізніше, пізніше будемо говорити"
    notes: "Comparative form. Used for sequencing future events."
  - lemma: "післязавтра"
    translation: "the day after tomorrow"
    pos: "adverb"
    usage: "післязавтра я буду вдома"
    notes: "Compound word: після (after) + завтра (tomorrow)."
  - lemma: "вранці"
    translation: "in the morning"
    pos: "adverb"
    usage: "завтра вранці, я встаю вранці"
    notes: "Often combined with завтра for future morning plans."
  - lemma: "ввечері"
    translation: "in the evening"
    pos: "adverb"
    usage: "завтра ввечері, ввечері будемо читати"
    notes: "Often combined with завтра for future evening plans."
  - lemma: "подорож"
    translation: "trip, journey"
    pos: "noun"
    gender: "f"
    usage: "планувати подорож, мріяти про подорож"
    notes: "Feminine noun. Common in plan-making contexts."
  - lemma: "відпочивати"
    translation: "to rest, to relax"
    pos: "verb"
    aspect: "imperfective"
    usage: "буду відпочивати вдома, відпочивати на вихідних"
    notes: "Common verb for weekend/vacation plans."
  - lemma: "вихідні"
    translation: "weekend"
    pos: "noun"
    usage: "плани на вихідні, на вихідних"
```

## Friction Constraints (Past Review Findings)

FRICTION CONSTRAINTS (from past build reviews — DO NOT repeat these errors):
- [GLOBAL] сес-тра is a VALID word division per Правопис 2019 §49. Do NOT mark it as an error. Phonetic syllabification (се-стра) and typographic word division (сес-тра) follow different rules — both are correct in their respective contexts.
- [GLOBAL] Ukrainian textbooks teach a hands-on-EARS test for voicing (закрий долонями вуха), NOT a hand-on-throat test. The hand-on-throat test is a valid phonetics technique but must NOT be attributed to Ukrainian textbooks. Source: Кравцова 2019, Grade 2, p.39.
- [GLOBAL] Do NOT invent Ukrainian words for minimal pairs. "Сір" is NOT a word meaning "grey" — the correct form is "сірий". Use verified minimal pairs only: кит/кіт, бити/біти, лис/ліс.

## Tier-Specific Guidance

## Review Protocol

### STEP 1: Language Quality Scan
- Every Ukrainian sentence: grammar, case endings, verb aspects, preposition agreement
- Russianisms: check against the checklist above AND scan for unlisted ones
- Calques: робити сенс→мати сенс, брати місце→відбуватися, etc.
- Natural Ukrainian phrasing vs translated-from-English constructions
- VESUM failures from pre-screen: are they valid forms or errors?

### STEP 2: Pedagogical Flow
- Does the lesson build progressively (simple → complex)?
- Are examples relevant to the grammar/vocabulary being taught?
- Would a beginner understand each step? ("Would I Continue?" test)
- Are activities well-designed and error-free?

### STEP 3: Auto-Fail Checklist
- Russianisms (кушать→їсти, приймати участь→брати участь, etc.)
- Colonial framing (Ukrainian defined by contrast with Russian)
- Word salad (unrelated claims strung together)
- Grammar scope violations (teaching concepts from later modules)
- Activity errors (wrong answers, impossible tasks)

### STEP 3a: Colonial Framing Check
Flag: "Unlike Russian...", "Different from Russian...", defining Ukrainian via Russian's absence.
Exceptions: `[!myth-buster]` debunking propaganda, `[!decolonization]` blocks, historical context.

### STEP 3b: LLM Fingerprint Scan
- Structural monotony: do 3+ sections start the same way?
- Example batching: 3+ sections with identical example formatting?
- Generic AI rhetoric: "це не просто", stacked abstract nouns, AI clichés
- Callout monotony: 3+ callouts with the same title?

### STEP 4: Score 7 Dimensions

| # | Dimension | Weight | Auto-fail |
|---|-----------|--------|-----------|
| 1 | Experience Quality | 1.5 | <7 |
| 2 | Language | 1.1 | <8 |
| 3 | Pedagogy | 1.2 | <7 |
| 4 | Activities | 1.3 | <7 |
| 5 | Beginner Safety | 1.3 | <7 |
| 6 | LLM Fingerprint | 1.0 | <7 |
| 7 | Linguistic Accuracy | 1.5 | <9 |

**Weighted Overall:**
```
Overall = (Experience x 1.5 + Language x 1.1 + Pedagogy x 1.2 +
          Activities x 1.3 + Beginner_Safety x 1.3 + LLM x 1.0 +
          Linguistic_Accuracy x 1.5) / 8.9
```

**Why 7 dimensions?** A1/A2 modules are short and topic-constrained, so Coherence, Relevance, Educational, Immersion, Richness, and Factual Accuracy are noise at this level — they auto-pass trivially and waste reviewer attention. Focus scoring on what actually differentiates good beginner modules.

# Tomorrow - Future Tense

*Talking About What Will Happen*

## Вступ (Introduction)

You already know how to talk about the present — **Я читаю книгу** (I'm reading a book). In the last module, you learned to look back at **вчора** (yesterday) and say what happened — **Я читав книгу** (I read a book). Now it's time to look forward to **завтра** (tomorrow).

Think of it as a timeline you've been building:

- **Вчора** я читав книгу. — Yesterday I read a book.
- **Сьогодні** я читаю книгу. — Today I'm reading a book.
- **Завтра** я **буду** читати книгу. — Tomorrow I will read a book.

Notice the pattern? The verb **читати** stays the same — what changes is the helper word before it. That little word **буду** is your key to the future.

There's a phrase Ukrainians use that captures the spirit of looking forward: **«Завтра буде новий день.»** — "Tomorrow will be a new day." You'll hear this in messages, in conversations, in songs. It's an expression of hope and resilience — no matter what happened today, tomorrow brings something new. And it's also a perfect example of how the future tense works: **буде** (will be) + the rest of the sentence.

By the end of this module, you'll be able to:
- Form the future tense using **буду** + infinitive
- Talk about your plans for tomorrow, next week, and beyond
- Express intentions using **хотіти** and **збиратися**
- Use time expressions like **завтра вранці** and **наступного тижня**

[!tip] **Quick win!**
You already know dozens of verbs in their infinitive form — **читати**, **писати**, **говорити**, **робити**. To make any of them future tense, just add the right form of **буду** in front. You're about to unlock the future with words you already know!

## Презентація граматики (Grammar Presentation)

### Складений майбутній час (Compound Future)

The compound future tense in Ukrainian works like a team of two words: a form of **бути** (to be) + the infinitive of the main verb. The helper verb changes to match the person — the main verb stays in the infinitive.

<!-- adapted from: Кравцова 2021, Grade 4, p.109 -->

| Особа | Однина | | Множина | |
|-------|--------|------|---------|------|
| 1st | я **буду** читати | I will read | ми **будемо** читати | we will read |
| 2nd | ти **будеш** читати | you will read | ви **будете** читати | you (pl.) will read |
| 3rd | він/вона **буде** читати | he/she will read | вони **будуть** читати | they will read |

The main verb never changes — only **бути** conjugates. Here's that same pattern with a few verbs you know:

- Я **буду** працювати. — I will work.
- Ти **будеш** говорити. — You will speak.
- Вона **буде** слухати музику. — She will listen to music.
- Ми **будемо** вивчати українську. — We will study Ukrainian.
- Ви **будете** читати книгу. — You will read a book.
- Вони **будуть** грати. — They will play.

<!-- adapted from: Варзацька 2021, Grade 4, p.133 -->

[!warning] **Learner Error — Aspect Conflict!**
**Буду** works ONLY with imperfective verbs (the ones you've been using — **читати**, **писати**, **робити**). Never combine **буду** with a perfective verb:
- **буду читати** ✅ (I will read / I will be reading)
- ~~буду прочитати~~ ❌ (incorrect — aspect clash!)

At A1, stick with the verbs you already know. They're all imperfective, so you're safe. If the infinitive ends in **-ати**, **-ити**, **-увати**, **-ти** and you've learned it in present tense — it works with **буду**.

### Synthetic Future (FYI — Recognition Only)

You may encounter a shorter future form in songs, news, or signs: **працюватиму**, **читатимеш**, **робитиме**. This is the synthetic future — the endings **-му**, **-меш**, **-ме**, **-мемо**, **-мете**, **-муть** are attached directly to the infinitive.

- **Я працюватиму** = Я буду працювати (I will work)
- **Він читатиме** = Він буде читати (He will read)

[!note] **Just recognise, don't memorise.**
Both forms mean the same thing. At this stage, use the compound form (**буду** + infinitive) when you speak and write. You'll learn to produce the synthetic form later — for now, just know it exists so you're not surprised when you see it.

## Лексика та культурний контекст (Vocabulary and Culture)

### Future Time Expressions

You already know **вчора** (yesterday) and **сьогодні** (today). Now let's add the forward-looking words:

- **Завтра** буде гарний день. — Tomorrow will be a nice day.
- **Завтра вранці** я буду пити каву. — Tomorrow morning I'll drink coffee.
- **Завтра вдень** ми будемо працювати. — Tomorrow afternoon we'll work.
- **Завтра ввечері** вони будуть читати. — Tomorrow evening they'll read.
- **Післязавтра** я буду вдома. — The day after tomorrow I'll be at home.
- **Скоро** буде літо. — Soon it will be summer.
- **Потім** я буду їсти. — Then I'll eat.
- **Пізніше** ми будемо говорити. — Later we'll talk.

### Конструкція «наступного тижня/року»

In English you say "next week" — the word "next" stays the same. In Ukrainian, **наступний** changes its ending when used with time expressions, and the noun changes too:

- **наступного тижня** — next week
- **наступного року** — next year
- **наступного разу** — next time
- **наступного місяця** — next month

[!warning] **Learner Error — Don't translate word-for-word!**
English "next week" keeps both words in their base form. Ukrainian uses a special form for both words: **наступного тижня** (not ~~наступний тиждень~~). Memorise these as fixed phrases — they use the Genitive case, which you'll study in more detail later.

Let's see them in sentences:

- **Наступного тижня** я буду багато працювати. — Next week I'll work a lot.
- **Наступного року** ми будемо вивчати більше. — Next year we'll study more.

### Plans and Intentions

Ukrainian has three ways to talk about the future, each with a different level of certainty:

| Expression | Meaning | Certainty |
|-----------|---------|-----------|
| **буду** + infinitive | I will... | confident plan |
| **збираюся** + infinitive | I'm going to... | intention |
| **хочу** + infinitive | I want to... | desire |

Let's compare:

- Я **буду** читати книгу. — I will read a book. (it's decided)
- Я **збираюся** читати книгу. — I'm going to read a book. (that's my plan)
- Я **хочу** читати книгу. — I want to read a book. (wish, not sure yet)

And there are two more verbs for softer future plans:

- Я **сподіваюся**, що завтра буде тепло. — I hope that tomorrow will be warm.
- Вона **мріє** про подорож. — She dreams about a trip.
- Ми **плануємо** вивчати українську. — We plan to study Ukrainian.

> **(At the office / На роботі)**
>
> — Які у тебе **плани** на вихідні? — What are your plans for the weekend?
> — Я **збираюся** їхати до Львова. — I'm going to go to Lviv.
> — О, добре! А **скоро** їдеш? — Oh, nice! Going soon?
> — **Завтра вранці**. А ти? — Tomorrow morning. And you?
> — Я **буду** відпочивати вдома. — I'll rest at home.
> — А **потім**? — And then?
> — **Пізніше** буду читати книгу. — Later I'll read a book.

[!culture] **«Не кажи "гоп", поки не перескочиш»**
Literally: "Don't say 'hop' until you've jumped over." Ukrainians tend to be cautious about announcing plans too confidently. You might hear someone use **збираюся** or **сподіваюся** even when they're quite sure — it's considered polite not to tempt fate. This is why knowing the difference between **буду** (certain), **збираюся** (planning), and **хочу** (hoping) matters beyond just grammar!

The word **план** is very common in everyday conversation:

- **мати плани** — to have plans
- **складати план** — to make a plan
- **плани на майбутнє** — plans for the future

> **(At a café / У кафе)**
>
> — Ти маєш **плани** на завтра? — Do you have plans for tomorrow?
> — Так! Я **хочу** їхати до парку. — Yes! I want to go to the park.
> — А **наступного тижня**? — And next week?
> — **Наступного тижня** буду працювати. — Next week I'll work.
> — Я теж. **Мрію** про канікули! — Me too. I dream about a holiday!
> — **Сподіваюся**, **скоро** будуть канікули. — I hope there'll be a holiday soon.

## Практика та підсумок (Practice and Summary)

Let's put everything together. You now have all the pieces to talk about your future:

**Step 1: Choose your time expression**
- **Завтра**, **завтра вранці**, **скоро**, **потім**, **пізніше**, **наступного тижня**

**Step 2: Add the right form of бути**
- **Я буду**, **ти будеш**, **він/вона буде**, **ми будемо**, **ви будете**, **вони будуть**

**Step 3: Add the infinitive**
- **читати**, **працювати**, **їхати**, **вивчати**, **відпочивати**...

Try building your own sentences with this formula:

- **Завтра** я **буду** _____________.
- **Наступного тижня** ми **будемо** _____________.
- Я **збираюся** _____________ **завтра ввечері**.

> **(Planning a trip / Планування подорожі)**
>
> — Давай **планувати** вихідні! — Let's plan the weekend!
> — Добре. У суботу я **буду** ходити до музею. — OK. On Saturday I'll go to the museum.
> — А я **збираюся** їхати на екскурсію. — And I'm going to go on a tour.
> — У неділю ми **будемо** разом? — On Sunday will we be together?
> — Так! Я **планую** бути вдома. — Yes! I plan to be at home.
> — А **потім** **будемо** дивитися фільм? — And then we'll watch a film?
> — **Сподіваюся**! Буде чудово. — I hope so! It'll be wonderful.

[!practice] **Self-check — Can you do these?**
- ✅ Conjugate **бути** in the future for all six persons
- ✅ Form sentences like **Я буду читати завтра**
- ✅ Use time expressions: **завтра вранці**, **наступного тижня**, **скоро**
- ✅ Choose between **буду** (certain), **збираюся** (plan), and **хочу** (wish)
- ✅ Recognise the synthetic future form: **працюватиму** = **буду працювати**

You've just unlocked the future tense! You can now talk about what you did yesterday, what you're doing today, and what you'll do tomorrow. **Завтра буде новий день** — and you're ready for it.

---

**Recommended video:** [Making plans — Future tense in Ukrainian (ULP 1-28)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eoIgON1cPcI) — Watch Anna and her guest plan their weekend using exactly the structures you've just learned.

**Recommended video:** [Quick Ukrainian Class #18: Future Verb Conjugation](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OF-4hhOcx58) — A quick walkthrough of **бути** conjugation with clear examples.

**Further reading:** [Future Tense in Ukrainian](https://www.ukrainianlessons.com/grammar-future/) — A comprehensive guide covering both compound and synthetic forms.

**Podcast:** [ULP S3 Ep96: Perfective future tense](https://www.ukrainianlessons.com/episode96/) — Listen to future tense forms in a natural conversation about New Year celebrations.

===REVIEW_START===
**Reviewed-By:** gemini-2.5-pro (RAG-grounded)

**Status:** FAIL
**Overall Score:** 7.9/10

## Scores

| # | Dimension | Score | Auto-fail | Evidence |
|---|-----------|-------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | Experience Quality | 8/10 | <7 | The explanations are generally friendly and encouraging, but the English calques and hallucinated idioms slightly detract from the authentic Ukrainian experience. |
| 2 | Language | 7/10 | <8 | Found a direct English calque ("Ти маєш плани") and a Russian imperative calque ("Давай планувати"). This triggers an auto-fail for A1 Language. |
| 3 | Pedagogy | 7/10 | <7 | The module introduces complex, unlearned grammar in a proverb (imperative `кажи`, perfective `перескочиш`), breaking the A1 pedagogical scaffolding. |
| 4 | Activities | 9/10 | <7 | Well-constructed fill-in-the-blank exercises with good distractor options that accurately target the lesson's grammar. |
| 5 | Beginner Safety | 8/10 | <7 | Mostly safe and well-paced, but the inclusion of the aforementioned proverb exposes beginners to confusing out-of-scope grammar. |
| 6 | LLM Fingerprint | 7/10 | <7 | Contains a hallucinated cultural claim describing the direct translation of "Tomorrow is a new day" as a unique phrase that "captures the spirit" of Ukrainians. |
| 7 | Linguistic Accuracy | 9/10 | <9 | The grammatical explanations regarding the compound vs. synthetic future and the aspect clash warning are highly accurate and well-framed. |

**Weighted Overall:** (8*1.5 + 7*1.1 + 7*1.2 + 9*1.3 + 8*1.3 + 7*1.0 + 9*1.5) / 8.9 = **7.9/10**

## Critical Issues Found

### Issue 1: Russianism & Imperative Violation
**Location**: Практика та підсумок (Practice and Summary)
**Problem**: The dialogue line `— Давай **планувати** вихідні!` uses "Давай" + infinitive, which is a calque from Russian ("давай планировать"). Furthermore, it violates the A1 morphology scope since the imperative form "давай" is not taught until M47 (as caught by the pre-screen).
**Fix**: Change the phrase to a natural, A1-appropriate alternative such as `— Час планувати вихідні!` (Time to plan the weekend!).

### Issue 2: English Calque (Anglicism)
**Location**: Лексика та культурний контекст (Vocabulary and Culture)
**Problem**: The dialogue line `— Ти маєш **плани** на завтра?` is an English calque ("You have plans"). While grammatically permissible, it is highly unnatural. Native speakers overwhelmingly use "У тебе є...".
**Fix**: Change the sentence to `— У тебе є плани на завтра?`

### Issue 3: Out-of-Scope Grammar in Proverb
**Location**: Лексика та культурний контекст (Vocabulary and Culture)
**Problem**: The `[!culture]` box introduces the proverb `«Не кажи "гоп", поки не перескочиш»`. This exposes A1 learners to the imperative (`кажи`) and perfective future (`перескочиш`), both of which are way out of scope and explicitly flagged by the pre-screen as violating beginner scaffolding.
**Fix**: Remove the proverb to maintain beginner safety. Keep the excellent cultural explanation about Ukrainians being cautious with plans.

### Issue 4: LLM Hallucinated Cultural Claim
**Location**: Вступ (Introduction)
**Problem**: The text claims: `There's a phrase Ukrainians use that captures the spirit of looking forward: **«Завтра буде новий день.»**` This is a classic LLM hallucination—taking a global/English idiom ("Tomorrow is a new day") and framing it as a profound, culturally unique Ukrainian saying.
**Fix**: Remove the exaggerated "captures the spirit" claim and present the sentence simply as a good, encouraging example of the future tense.

### Issue 5: Unnatural Phrasing in Dialogue
**Location**: Лексика та культурний контекст (Vocabulary and Culture)
**Problem**: The question `— О, добре! А **скоро** їдеш?` (Oh, nice! Going soon?) sounds clunky and translated.
**Fix**: A native speaker would naturally ask `— О, добре! **Коли** їдеш?` (When are you going?).

## Ukrainian Language Issues

- `Давай планувати` — Russianism and A1 morphology violation.
- `Ти маєш плани` — English calque.
- `А скоро їдеш?` — Unnatural phrasing.

## Fix Plan to Reach PASS

1. Replace "Давай планувати" with "Час планувати".
2. Change "Ти маєш плани" to "У тебе є плани".
3. Remove the "Не кажи гоп..." proverb but retain the cultural explanation in the callout.
4. Rewrite the introduction to remove the hallucinated cultural claim about "Завтра буде новий день".
5. Change "А скоро їдеш?" to "Коли їдеш?".

## Verification Summary

- Read module markdown, activities YAML, and vocabulary YAML completely.
- Checked vocabulary list against VESUM flags (all failed words like `Львова` were verified as valid proper nouns or noun forms).
- Verified Russianisms and Anglicisms against A1 constraints.
- Vocabulary Items Found (20): завтра, бути, наступний, план, хотіти, збиратися, скоро, потім, тиждень, рік, сподіватися, мріяти, планувати, пізніше, післязавтра, вранці, ввечері, подорож, відпочивати, вихідні.
- Activities Found (1): Complete the Future Tense (fill-in with 20 items). No issues found with the activity structure or distractor options.

## Verdict

**Status:** FAIL
**Rationale:** The module provides a solid grammatical foundation, but it fails on the Language and LLM Fingerprint dimensions due to Russian/English calques ("Давай планувати", "Ти маєш плани") and hallucinated cultural claims. It also violates A1 morphology rules by introducing unexplained imperatives in a proverb and dialogue.
===REVIEW_END===

FILE: /Users/krisztiankoos/projects/learn-ukrainian/curriculum/l2-uk-en/a1/tomorrow-future-tense.md
FIND:
There's a phrase Ukrainians use that captures the spirit of looking forward: **«Завтра буде новий день.»** — "Tomorrow will be a new day." You'll hear this in messages, in conversations, in songs. It's an expression of hope and resilience — no matter what happened today, tomorrow brings something new. And it's also a perfect example of how the future tense works: **буде** (will be) + the rest of the sentence.
REPLACE:
You can already make a simple sentence about the future: **«Завтра буде новий день.»** — "Tomorrow will be a new day." It's an expression of hope — no matter what happened today, tomorrow brings something new. And it's also a perfect example of how the future tense works: **буде** (will be) + the rest of the sentence.
---
FIND:
> **(At the office / На роботі)**
>
> — Які у тебе **плани** на вихідні? — What are your plans for the weekend?
> — Я **збираюся** їхати до Львова. — I'm going to go to Lviv.
> — О, добре! А **скоро** їдеш? — Oh, nice! Going soon?
> — **Завтра вранці**. А ти? — Tomorrow morning. And you?
REPLACE:
> **(At the office / На роботі)**
>
> — Які у тебе **плани** на вихідні? — What are your plans for the weekend?
> — Я **збираюся** їхати до Львова. — I'm going to go to Lviv.
> — О, добре! **Коли** їдеш? — Oh, nice! When are you going?
> — **Завтра вранці**. А ти? — Tomorrow morning. And you?
---
FIND:
> **(At a café / У кафе)**
>
> — Ти маєш **плани** на завтра? — Do you have plans for tomorrow?
> — Так! Я **хочу** їхати до парку. — Yes! I want to go to the park.
REPLACE:
> **(At a café / У кафе)**
>
> — У тебе є **плани** на завтра? — Do you have plans for tomorrow?
> — Так! Я **хочу** їхати до парку. — Yes! I want to go to the park.
---
FIND:
[!culture] **«Не кажи "гоп", поки не перескочиш»**
Literally: "Don't say 'hop' until you've jumped over." Ukrainians tend to be cautious about announcing plans too confidently. You might hear someone use **збираюся** or **сподіваюся** even when they're quite sure — it's considered polite not to tempt fate. This is why knowing the difference between **буду** (certain), **збираюся** (planning), and **хочу** (hoping) matters beyond just grammar!
REPLACE:
[!culture] **Don't Tempt Fate!**
Ukrainians tend to be cautious about announcing plans too confidently. You might hear someone use **збираюся** (I am going to) or **сподіваюся** (I hope) even when they are quite sure — it's considered polite not to tempt fate. This is why knowing the difference between **буду** (certain), **збираюся** (planning), and **хочу** (hoping) matters beyond just grammar!
---
FIND:
> **(Planning a trip / Планування подорожі)**
>
> — Давай **планувати** вихідні! — Let's plan the weekend!
> — Добре. У суботу я **буду** ходити до музею. — OK. On Saturday I'll go to the museum.
REPLACE:
> **(Planning a trip / Планування подорожі)**
>
> — Час **планувати** вихідні! — Time to plan the weekend!
> — Добре. У суботу я **буду** ходити до музею. — OK. On Saturday I'll go to the museum.
